<!-- Generated by a web-grounded research workflow (8 interview frameworks, parallel -> synthesis), 2026-06-13. Spot-check any exact statistic before quoting it in the live pitch. -->

# HIGH-ROI QUESTIONS — the highest-signal asks for DOT

> The prompt-bible for the **Check-in Generator** and **Clinical Shaper** agents (`EXTRACTION-ORCHESTRATION.md` §3), and the question-design source for the **Safety Sentinel** and **Reflection Composer**. Every ask below is rewritten in DOT's locked voice (`VOICE-AND-TONE.md`): lowercase, warm, one-thing-at-a-time, validate-first, **invisible instrument** — the clinical structure lives only in the provider artifact, never on the user's screen. The clinical wording is here so the agents preserve the validated *construct* and recall window; the DOT-voice column is what the user actually sees.
>
> Ranked across **all** frameworks by signal-per-question, not within them. The crisis sequence (§3) is held separate because it is the one place DOT's "describe-never-diagnose" posture yields to a hard escalation rule.

---

## 1. The ranked top high-ROI questions

Ranked most signal-per-question first. "Tier" groups them: **S = safety-critical** · **A = openers/agenda** · **B = the fear underneath** · **C = function & delta** · **D = motivation/trust multipliers**. The full safe-ask sequence is detailed in §3; here only its single load-bearing item appears, so the table reads as one ranking.

| # | Question (clinical) | What it unlocks | Why high-ROI | DOT-voice version |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | *In the past week, have you been having thoughts about killing yourself?* (ASQ-3 / C-SSRS Q2 — active ideation) | The active-ideation gate: converts a soft worry into the clinical fork that determines everything downstream. | **The highest-stakes question that exists.** A single binary flips disposition from "journal" to "get help now." Evidence says asking is safe (does not increase ideation). "Killing yourself" is the validated word — vague terms under-detect. | *and lately — has your mind actually gone to the thought of ending your life? not "should i," just… has the thought shown up? you can be straight with me.* |
| 2 | *What about the situation troubles you the most?* (BATHE-T) | The core fear / the person's OWN ranking of what matters — usually different from the presenting complaint. | The single highest-ROI question in any non-safety framework. One sentence yields the real reason-for-visit and the fear under it. Evidence: surfaced "I'm going to die on my own when I'm getting breathless" where the complaint was breathlessness. It is also the gentlest on-ramp to risk. | *of all of it — what's the part that scares you most, or sits heaviest? the thing your brain keeps going back to.* |
| 3 | *What's been going on? / what problems brought you in today?* (open HPI opener — then **silence**, let them finish) | The true presenting agenda, in their own words and order, before it filters into symptom-codes. | Highest-yield single *move* in clinical interviewing. Clinicians interrupt at ~18s; only 23% of patients ever finish; letting them finish costs ≤150s. One open opener + silence out-yields a dozen closed questions, and it's how a minimizer's real story emerges. | *hey — what's been going on with you lately? take your time, no rush. i'm just here to listen.* |
| 4 | *Is there something else you want to address today?* (agenda — **"something," not "anything"**) | The withheld, often more important concern — otherwise a late "doorknob" disclosure or never voiced. | The single highest-ROI *wording* in the literature. RCT: "something else" eliminated 78% of unmet concerns (OR .154); "anything else" was indistinguishable from asking nothing — and it added zero time. One word flips the door open. | *is there something else that's been on your mind too? even if it feels unrelated or kind of small — now's a good time.* |
| 5 | *What is the event prompting your emotion? Describe just the facts — challenge any judgments or absolutes.* (DBT Check-the-Facts step 2) | The clean, sense-based objective event with interpretation stripped — the Objective half DOT computes every delta against. | The literal fork between Subjective and Objective. Without this split there is no delta to surface; with it, every distortion becomes visible by contrast. Also catches absolute language ("always," "everyone") that flags distortion in the wording itself. | *ok walk me through what actually happened — just the play-by-play, like you're describing a clip. who was there, what got said. we'll get to how it felt in a sec.* |
| 6 | *What are you most worried it could be? / what's the feared version?* (ICE-Concerns) | The named catastrophic fear they came to rule out but may never say unprompted ("I'm scared this is a tumour / that I'm losing my mind"). | Concerns surface in only ~55% of consults and the clinician asks in ~3% — a high-miss, high-value gap a tool reliably closes. Naming the feared thing is also the top red-flag triage cue. For DOT, this is the catastrophizing the objective record gets held against. | *is there a specific scary version of this you've been quietly worried about? you can name the worst one — most people have one they haven't said out loud.* |
| 7 | *How's this affecting your day-to-day — sleep, work, the people around you, taking care of yourself?* (ICEE effects / functional impact) | Functional impact as lived — the objective spine of severity (sleep, work, relationships, self-care). | The de-minimizer. It's the rarest component to surface naturally (~42%) yet the most decision-relevant. Converts "I'm fine" into "scratching my arms in meetings" and "want to sleep forever" — concrete facts a provider can act on. Same construct as the GAD-7/PHQ-9 impairment item. | *how's all this been hitting your actual days? like — sleep, getting stuff done, hanging with people, eating? give me a real example from this week.* |
| 8 | *Does your emotion, and/or its intensity, fit the actual facts?* (DBT Check-the-Facts step 6) | The person's OWN read on whether the size of the feeling matches the size of the event — the felt-vs-actual delta, stated by them. | The closing move that lands the whole sequence **without DOT issuing a verdict** — the framework hands the conclusion to the person. Separates "the feeling is valid" (always true) from "the intensity matches the facts" (the checkable part). Exactly DOT's "validate first, never invalidate." | *no wrong answer — looking at just the facts, does the size of the feeling kinda match the size of the thing? both can be true: the feeling's real AND it might be bigger than the moment. just noticing, not grading.* |
| 9 | *What thoughts have you had about what might be going on?* (ICE-Ideas — open "telling," not polar) | The person's own explanatory model, with the real fear embedded inside it. | Highest base-rate yield of any ICE prong (ideas appear ~79%) and the strongest predictor of satisfaction (OR 10.74). Wording is load-bearing: "what thoughts have you had" presupposes thoughts exist; "do you have any ideas" invites a dead-end "no." | *before anything else — what's your own gut feeling about what's going on? even if it sounds silly. i actually want the version in your head.* |
| 10 | *Why now — what made you reach out today rather than last week?* (precipitant / "why now") | The trigger that converted ongoing distress into help-seeking — localizes the inflection point and any acute stressor. | One question separates a chronic baseline from an acute escalation — critical for severity AND risk (situational stressors are named suicide precipitants). The gentlest doorway to the trigger event that anchors the whole timeline. | *what made today the day you wanted to talk about it? did something happen, or did it just kind of build up? totally okay either way.* |
| 11 | *How do you feel about that?* (BATHE-A) | The actual emotional state, named in their own words — the gap between what happened and how it landed. | Affect is the best lever to distinguish situational stress from clinical anxiety/depression, and answers are "often unexpected" — you can't infer feeling from facts. Doubles as validation: naming the emotion is itself the thing most people never get asked. | *and how'd that feel for you? whatever the honest word is — even if it's a messy one or "i don't know."* |
| 12 | *What's the worst that could happen? … and if it did, how would you cope?* (CBT decatastrophizing) | The bottom-of-the-fear AND their coping resources — the missing piece that makes dread feel survivable. | Highest-ROI for reaching the fear underneath. Most people stop at the edge of the catastrophe; pairing "worst case" with "how would you handle it" reliably drops intensity because the unspoken belief was "I couldn't survive it." **Never run on a real threat — see red lines.** | *if it all went as bad as it could — what's the worst-case in your head? … okay, say that happened. what would you do the day after? you've gotten through hard things before.* |
| 13 | *Over the last 2 weeks — has the stuff you usually enjoy just not done it for you?* (PHQ-2 anhedonia) | Anhedonia — a DSM gateway symptom, harder to rationalize than "sadness," and the one most omitted from a free-text journal. | Half of the PHQ-2 (sens ~83% / spec ~90% at ≥3). Catches the flat, numb depression people don't label as "down." Adds signal a story rarely volunteers. | *be honest — lately, have the things you usually like just… not done it for you? like the spark's kind of gone?* |
| 14 | *When the worry starts, can you stop or control it?* (GAD-2 uncontrollability) | Uncontrollability of worry — the discriminating feature of clinical anxiety (worry that runs you vs. worry you can set down). | The highest-yield GAD-2 item; GAD-2 triages all four anxiety disorders (AUC .80–.91). Everyone worries — *can't stop* is the pathological marker. | *when the worry starts up, can you usually steer out of it — or does it kind of grab the wheel and not let go?* |
| 15 | *Have you felt that you or your family would be better off if you were dead?* (ASQ-2 burdensomeness) | Perceived burdensomeness — the "they'd be better off without me" belief, a core driver of suicidal desire, often felt before any plan. | Reaches a distinct, dangerous cognition the death-wish item misses, and taps the shame people are least likely to voice. A safety-screen item (full sequence §3). | *does part of you ever feel like the people around you would have it easier if you weren't in the picture? a lot of people carry that and never say it.* |
| 16 | *0–10, how important is it to change this? … why not lower? … what would nudge it up one?* (MI change-ruler) | Quantifies motivation, then makes the person voice their OWN reasons (anchor **down**) and barriers. | The crown-jewel MI move: a number is cheap and non-threatening, the follow-ups convert it into self-motivating speech. Reusable for importance/confidence/desire by swapping the verb. **Anchor downward only** — "why not higher" elicits resistance. | *honestly, 0 to 10, how much do you want things to change right now? … why a [number] and not lower? … what'd nudge it up even one?* |
| 17 | *How are you handling that?* (BATHE-H) | Current coping and adaptive capacity — strengths inventory AND the can't-cope warning sign. | Surfaces function and coping, AND flags the at-risk person ("I can't handle it"). One question yields both the protective factors and the distress flag. | *how are you getting through the days with it? even the small stuff — what's keeping you going, or what's stopped working?* |
| 18 | *Tell me more about that. / what does that look like day to day?* (MI / facilitation deepener) | Depth on whatever they just opened — turns "it's been rough" into dateable, function-level detail. | The cheapest high-yield move: adds zero new agenda, follows their lead, near-zero resistance. Safest possible prompt for a minimizer — you're already on their topic — and it's how a label becomes lived specifics. | *wait, say more about that — what does that actually look like day to day?* |
| 19 | *It took something to come here and say this.* (MI affirmation — a statement) | Not data — the trust accelerant that makes every question above answerable. Named strengths are protective factors for the record. | The multiplier on every other question's yield; without it the high-yield asks get guarded, minimized answers. **Must be specific and earned** — generic praise reads as flattery and lowers signal. | *honestly, just naming this stuff takes guts. i can tell you've been holding a lot — glad you're putting it down here.* |
| 20 | *That must be very difficult for you.* (BATHE-E — a statement, the validation close) | Closes the loop: signals the disclosure was received, which is what keeps an anxious person disclosing rather than minimizing. | The trust multiplier patients tied directly to feeling "heard." Costs one line and protects the honesty of every answer above. For DOT's validate-first register, the load-bearing move on every turn. | *that's genuinely a lot to be carrying. it makes complete sense that it's been hard.* |

---

## 2. Mapping onto DOT's experiential flow

The flow (`VOICE-AND-TONE.md` §8): **enter → talk → reflect → check-ins → logs → summary.** Each question fires at the phase where its signal is safe to ask and most useful. The "invisible-instrument rewording" is what the user sees; the captured value preserves the validated construct + recall window for the provider artifact.

| Phase | What's happening | Questions that fire (by #) | Invisible-instrument rewording (user-facing) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Enter** | You just talk. No form, no clinical screen. Lowest activation. | **#3** open opener (then silence) | *"i'm here to help you put what you're feeling into words — your way. just talk. what's been going on?"* — then **stay quiet and let them finish.** No follow-up until the opening statement is complete. |
| **Talk** | DOT listens, mirrors words, validates first. Disclosure decouples from "I'm using a mental-health app." | **#11** affect → **#9** ideas → **#18** deepener → **#10** why-now → **#2** the trouble/fear → **#6** the feared version → **#4** agenda-sweep | Interleave with their own cues — **never fire back-to-back like a form.** Mirror their word back before the next ask ("a panic attack a day… tell me more about those"). Validate (**#20**) before probing. Use "what thoughts have you had" / "something else," never the polar/"anything else" forms. |
| **Reflect** | The two-way delta, surfaced neutrally, validate-first. | **#5** event-vs-fact → **#8** does-the-size-match (handed to *them*) → **#12** worst-case + how-you'd-cope | Lead with the body and feeling ("the chest tightness is real") **before** any evidence. Present felt + recorded side by side and **stop** — #8 is the person's own observation, never DOT's verdict. Run at most the first two steps unless distress is low. |
| **Check-ins** | One grounded, adaptive ask at the right moment — the Check-in Generator's whole job. | **#7** function → **#13** anhedonia → **#14** uncontrollable worry → **#17** coping → **#16** the ruler (sparingly) | Grounded in what was surfaced: panic showed up → *"did you have a panic attack today, after the club thing?"*; exhaustion → *"did you get any real rest?"* **One at a time, opt-out implicit, never turn 1.** The captured value still maps to GAD-2/PHQ-2/PEG — invisible. |
| **Logs** | The record accumulates; each story is a glass dot. The objective counter-evidence accrues quietly. | (no new asks — the agenda-sweep **#4** re-probes here as trust builds) | A second, safer window once rapport exists: *"is there anything else sitting with you? even something you weren't sure you'd say."* Catches the door-handle disclosure. |
| **Summary** | The mirror + the provider artifact. | (Clinical Shaper reads back, does not ask) — strengths from **#17/#19** become protective factors | The provider summary de-minimizes (S/O only). On screen: the validate-first delta and the catch line. **No score, no label, ever shown.** |

**Cross-cutting wiring for the agents:**
- **Permission-giving lead-in** before any shame-loaded ask (self-harm, scratching, not eating): normalize the *act of reaching out*, never minimize the *feeling*. *"a lot of people going through a hard stretch get thoughts that scare them, or do stuff like scratch — no judgment at all if any of that's been you. has it?"*
- **Don't over-fire the sweep.** Re-probe "something else?" at most a couple of times, spaced by genuine engagement — stacking it reads as nagging and breaks the invisible-instrument feel.
- **The Safety Sentinel runs first, every turn** (§3). Any risk signal short-circuits the normal flow — it does **not** get a counter-evidence delta.

---

## 3. SAFETY — the direct safe-ask (highest-stakes, highest-ROI)

This is the one place DOT's "invisible instrument, describe-never-diagnose" posture **yields to a hard escalation rule.** These items *screen*; they do not assess or treat. The demo story contains real signals: self-harm behavior (*"scratching my arms during club meetings to keep myself calm"*) and passive ideation / exhaustion (*"all I want to do is sleep forever"*).

### 3a. The graded sequence (passive → active → intent → plan/means → acute → history)

Ask passive-before-active; a clean negative on the death-wish + active-ideation items is a valid stopping point — do not interrogate someone into distress. Keep the direct word ("kill / end your life"), just wrapped warmly. Each item's clinical source is in §5.

| Step | Clinical wording (ASQ / C-SSRS) | What it decides | DOT-voice safe-ask |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 — wish to be dead (passive) | *In the past few weeks, have you wished you were dead, or wished you could go to sleep and not wake up?* | Gentlest doorway; sorts "stop" vs "keep asking." | *lately, have there been moments you kind of wished you just… weren't here anymore? even quietly. no wrong answer here.* |
| 2 — burdensomeness | *Have you felt that you or your family would be better off if you were dead?* | Reaches the "I'm a burden" belief — the fear underneath. | *does part of you ever feel like the people around you would have it easier if you weren't in the picture? a lot of people carry that and never say it.* |
| 3 — **active ideation (the gate)** | *In the past week, have you been having thoughts about killing yourself?* | The clinical pivot. A yes opens steps 4–5. | *and lately — has your mind actually gone to the thought of ending your life? not "should i," just… has the thought shown up? you can be straight with me.* |
| 4 — intent | *Have you had some intention of acting on these thoughts?* | Separates rumination from leaning-toward-action — a strong near-term multiplier. | *when that thought comes up, does it feel like just a thought passing through — or does part of you actually mean it, like you might act on it?* |
| 5 — plan + means | *Have you worked out the details of how? Do you intend to carry it out? Is anything you'd use close by?* | Plan + available means + intent = emergency disposition. Also a means-restriction target. | *has your mind gone as far as a how — a specific way, or a time? and is anything you'd use close by right now? i'm asking because it matters, not to scare you.* |
| 6 — **acute ("right now")** | *Are you having thoughts of killing yourself right now?* | The escalation tier — emergency vs same-day. Cannot be inferred from any other answer. | *right now, in this moment — are you thinking about ending your life? whatever you say, i'm staying right here with you, and i can put real help in front of you immediately.* |
| 7 — prior attempt | *Have you ever tried to kill yourself? When?* | The strongest long-term predictor; reweights the whole picture. | *has there ever been a time you actually tried to end your life — even something you never told anyone? and if so, how long ago? no judgment, just want the real picture.* |

### 3b. The evidence that asking does NOT increase risk

This is why DOT asks directly instead of avoiding the topic — and the line for the deck/provider trust:
- **Meta-analysis (DeCou & Schumann, 2018, 13 studies):** *no significant iatrogenic effects of assessing suicidality*, including in high-risk groups — directly allaying the fear that asking plants the idea.
- **AAFP / VA-DoD review (2021):** asking patients at high risk about suicide does **not** increase ideation or attempts and leads to better outcomes; no studies found increased harm from screening.
- **Wording matters:** asking "killing yourself" rather than "hurting yourself" is recommended — patients treat self-harm and suicide as separate, so vague terms under-detect. A single question alone under-detects; a short graded sequence is preferred.

### 3c. Route-to-human escalation (the hard rule)

For DOT specifically — a **non-clinical tool** — a positive screen must **escalate, not just record**:
- **Trigger tiers.** Active ideation (step 3 yes) → surface crisis resources + route to a human. The **acute "right now"** item, a worked-out plan with access to means, or a recent prior attempt → **immediate, visible handoff**: **988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline** (call/text, US), **Crisis Text Line** ("HOME" to **741741**), **911 / ER** if imminent.
- **Architecture (`EXTRACTION-ORCHESTRATION.md` §3, `VOICE-AND-TONE.md` §6).** The **Safety Sentinel runs first, every turn**, high-recall, conservative (when unsure, flag). `risk=true` → the run **pauses at `awaiting-human`** (L4 `waitForSignal`); the user-facing reply is the **Reflection Composer in crisis mode** — care + route-to-human, **NOT** a counter-evidence delta.
- **Validate the pain; do NOT counter-evidence it.** Never answer *"I want to sleep forever"* with *"but your friends texted you."* Counter-evidence belongs to the *catastrophe* delta, never the crisis path. *"hey — the arm-scratching, and wanting to sleep forever — that sounds really heavy, and i want to make sure you're okay. you don't have to carry this alone. would it help to talk to someone? i can help you put it into words for them."*
- **No "safety contract."** Never use "promise me you won't" framing — it's ineffective and shuts down disclosure. Offer a collaborative crisis/safety plan and a supportive means-safety conversation ("putting time and distance between you and the method"), never confiscation or judgment.
- **The record still captures frequency for the provider** — factually, no diagnostic label. But the *user-facing* reply is care + routing. Never silently log acute risk and move on. **No confetti, no streak, no "nice job"** on a safety turn.
- **Minors / duty-to-protect:** a non-clinical tool defaults to surfacing crisis resources and a trusted adult/clinician — it does **not** make a containment decision itself.

---

## 4. The RED LINES (non-negotiable for every agent)

1. **DOT describes, never diagnoses.** These are *screens and elicitation tools*, not diagnoses. A positive PHQ-2 / GAD-2 / single-item NEVER means "you have depression/anxiety" — only "worth a fuller look / worth telling a clinician." Patient owns Subjective + Objective; clinician owns Assessment + Plan. DOT produces a *story*, never an A/P.
2. **Never score or label to the user.** No instrument names ("GAD-7," "PHQ-9," "C-SSRS"), no cutoffs, no "cognitive distortion / catastrophizing / mind-reading," no "score = condition" verdict — ever, in user-facing copy. The validated construct (anhedonia, uncontrollable worry, hopelessness, passive vs active ideation) and recall window live **only** in the provider artifact.
3. **Every instrument stays invisible.** The warm wording is the surface; the clinical structure is underneath. The casual rewrite must not drift the meaning — "do you ever worry" is NOT the GAD-2 "can't stop/control worrying"; preserve the construct even while sounding like a friend.
4. **Never a verdict; present and stop.** Surface the felt thing and the recorded thing side by side and **stop.** Never resolve the gap, never say which is true, never "so your anxiety is lying," never "you're overreacting." Check-the-Facts step 6 is handed to the user as *their* observation, never rendered by DOT as judgment.
5. **Validate FIRST, always — the body and feeling before any analysis.** The feeling is always valid even when its intensity may not fit the facts. An invalidating or clinical reply ruptures trust in one line and escalates anxiety. Normalize the *act of reaching out*, never minimize the *feeling*.
6. **Don't decatastrophize a real threat.** The "evidence against" and "how would you cope" moves can harm if the danger, abuse, or loss is genuine, or if distress is acute. Check that the gap is an *interpretation* gap before separating event from interpretation.
7. **Crisis overrides everything.** Any self-harm / ideation signal abandons the Socratic/delta frame entirely and routes to §3 — never weigh evidence against a safety signal, never counter-evidence despair.
8. **One thing at a time; never a checklist.** The frameworks are menus, not batteries. Running all the steps in one sitting reads as an interrogation and breaks the "texting one friend" register. One question per turn, one observation per beat, and let silence be valid.
9. **Never block the path to a human.** DOT surfaces resources and stays present; it never implies it is providing crisis care, triage, or clinical judgment.

---

## 5. SOURCES

Deduped, only sources present in the findings.

**BATHE**
- Stuart MR, Lieberman JA. *Psychotherapy in Primary Care: The BATHE Technique.* AAFP / Am Fam Physician, 1998. https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/1998/0501/p2131.html
- Stuart & Lieberman, *The BATHE Method* (PMC181054). https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC181054/
- *Acceptability of the BATHE technique amongst GPs and frequently attending patients in primary care: a nested qualitative study.* BMC Primary Care, 2019 (PMC6724282). https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6724282/

**ICE / ICEE (patient perspective)**
- Connell et al. *Ideas, concerns, expectations, and effects on life (ICEE) in GP consultations.* BJGP Open, 2023. https://bjgpopen.org/content/7/4/BJGPO.2023.0008 · PubMed: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37277172/
- Cals et al. *Patients' ideas, concerns, and expectations (ICE) in general practice: impact on prescribing.* PubMed 19105913. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19105913/
- Murtagh GM. *A critical look at ideas, concerns and expectations in clinical communication.* Medical Education, 2023;57(4):331–336 (DOI 10.1111/medu.14975). https://spiral.imperial.ac.uk/server/api/core/bitstreams/57c93618-ed52-442c-b6df-e2015d5c5986/content · https://asmepublications.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1111/medu.14975
- Geeky Medics, *ICE — OSCE Guide.* https://geekymedics.com/ice/ · My GP Notes: https://www.mygpnotes.com/gp-world/ideas-concerns-and-expectations-ice/

**Agenda-setting / "What Else?"**
- Heritage J, Robinson JD, Elliott MN, Beckett M, Wilkes M. *Reducing Patients' Unmet Concerns in Primary Care: the Difference One Word Can Make.* J Gen Intern Med, 2007;22(10):1429–33. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17674111/
- Marvel MK, Epstein RM, Flowers K, Beckman HB. *Soliciting the patient's agenda: have we improved?* JAMA, 1999;281(3):283–7. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/9918487/
- Baker LH, O'Connell D, Platt FW. *"What Else?" Setting the Agenda for the Clinical Interview.* Ann Intern Med, 2005;143(10):766–70. https://www.acpjournals.org/doi/10.7326/0003-4819-143-10-200511150-00033
- *Taxonomy and Effectiveness of Clinician Agenda-Setting Questions in Routine Ambulatory Encounters: A Mixed Method Study.* PMC12628284. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12628284/
- Singh Ospina N et al. *Eliciting the Patient's Agenda — Secondary Analysis of Recorded Clinical Encounters.* J Gen Intern Med, 2019. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6318197/ · https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11606-018-4540-5

**Motivational Interviewing (OARS + evoking change talk)**
- *Motivational Interviewing.* StatPearls, NCBI Bookshelf. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK589705/
- *TIP 35, Ch.3 — Motivational Interviewing as a Counseling Style* (Enhancing Motivation for Change in SUD Treatment), NCBI Bookshelf. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK571068/
- Eck-Maahs S. *Ten Strategies for Evoking Change Talk* (MINT / motivationalinterviewing.org). https://www.motivationalinterviewing.org/sites/default/files/Ten%20Strategies%20for%20Evoking%20Change%20Talk%20Sue%20EckMaahs.pdf
- MINT, *Understanding Motivational Interviewing.* https://motivationalinterviewing.org/understanding-motivational-interviewing
- Homeless Hub, *OARS.* https://homelesshub.ca/resource/motivational-interviewing-open-questions-affirmation-reflective-listening-and-summary-reflections-oars/

**CBT Socratic questioning + DBT Check the Facts**
- Linehan MM. *DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, 2nd ed.* — Emotion Regulation Handout 8, *Check the Facts* (©2015). https://in.nau.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/202/Check-the-Facts.pdf
- Bay Area DBT & CC, *Check the Facts.* https://bayareadbtcc.com/check-the-facts/
- SimplePractice, *Socratic Questioning in CBT.* https://www.simplepractice.com/blog/socratic-questioning-cbt/
- Positive Psychology, *CBT Cognitive Restructuring & Cognitive Distortions.* https://positivepsychology.com/cbt-cognitive-restructuring-cognitive-distortions/
- Simply Psychology, *Cognitive Distortions in CBT.* https://www.simplypsychology.org/cognitive-distortions-in-cbt.html

**Suicide-safe inquiry (ASQ / C-SSRS / single direct ask)**
- NIMH ASQ training. https://www.nimh.nih.gov/news/media/2019/suicide-risk-screening-training-how-to-use-the-asq-to-detect-patients-at-risk-for-suicide · ASQ tool PDF: https://www.nimh.nih.gov/sites/default/files/documents/research/research-conducted-at-nimh/asq-toolkit-materials/asq-tool/screening-tool-asq-nimh-toolkit.pdf · ASQ toolkit: https://www.nimh.nih.gov/research/research-conducted-at-nimh/asq-toolkit-materials · ASQ validation: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7855604/
- Columbia C-SSRS — Baseline/Screening PDF: https://cssrs.columbia.edu/wp-content/uploads/C-SSRS1-14-09-BaselineScreening.pdf · Columbia Protocol: https://cssrs.columbia.edu/wp-content/uploads/Columbia_Protocol.pdf · structure summary: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Columbia_Suicide_Severity_Rating_Scale
- AAP, *Screening for Suicide Risk in Clinical Practice.* https://www.aap.org/en/patient-care/blueprint-for-youth-suicide-prevention/strategies-for-clinical-settings-for-youth-suicide-prevention/screening-for-suicide-risk-in-clinical-practice/ · single-item misclassification: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4619664/
- AAFP, *The Suicidal Patient: Evaluation and Management.* Am Fam Physician, 2021. https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/2021/0401/p417.html
- DeCou CR, Schumann ME. *On the Iatrogenic Risk of Assessing Suicidality: A Meta-Analysis.* Suicide Life Threat Behav, 2018. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28678380/

**Ultra-brief validated screens (PHQ-2 / GAD-2 / PHQ-4 / Whooley / single-item)**
- Kroenke K, Spitzer RL, Williams JBW. *The Patient Health Questionnaire-2.* Med Care, 2003. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/14583691/
- Kroenke K et al. *Anxiety disorders in primary care (GAD-7/GAD-2).* Ann Intern Med, 2007. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17339617/
- Kroenke K, Spitzer RL, Williams JBW, Löwe B. *An ultra-brief screening scale for anxiety and depression: the PHQ-4* (2009). https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19996233/ · instrument text: https://www.recoveryanswers.org/assets/phq-4.pdf
- UCSF Whooley Questions (history). https://whooleyquestions.ucsf.edu/history · meta-analysis: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26656018/
- *Diagnostic utility of a one-item question to screen for depressive disorders — KORA F3.* https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3906912/

**Function-and-narrative (open HPI elicitation)**
- *The Medical Interview* — NCBI Clinical Methods. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK349/ (incl. Beckman & Frankel 1984)
- AAFP, *Geriatric Assessment: An Office-Based Approach.* 2018. https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/2018/0615/p776.html
- *Suicide Precipitants Across the Lifespan.* PMC5923733. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5923733/ · *Suicidal Ideation*, StatPearls: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK565877/
- *Interviewing people on sensitive topics: challenges and strategies.* Eur J Cardiovasc Nurs, 2025. https://academic.oup.com/eurjcn/article/24/3/488/7761136

---

*This doc is the question-design source for the Check-in Generator and Clinical Shaper agents (`docs/reference/EXTRACTION-ORCHESTRATION.md`) and informs the Safety Sentinel and Reflection Composer. The voice law is `docs/reference/VOICE-AND-TONE.md`; this doc supplies the WHAT-to-ask, that doc supplies the HOW-it-must-sound. Crisis routing is enforced architecturally (Safety Sentinel first, every turn → `awaiting-human` pause). Suggested path: `docs/reference/HIGH-ROI-QUESTIONS.md`.*
